[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2025-07-14
[TITLE]: Can cleaning up illegal dumps build community? Oakland volunteers sure hope so. - CSMonitor.com
[METADATA/INTRO]: ‘What community looks like’: Oakland volunteers haul away 25 tons of trash Loading......
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: “Random citizens and businesses would come dump their trash,” he says. “It really affected a lot of us because it looked like that’s what we were doing. But it really wasn’t.” 
 In a 2022 interview with the San Francisco Chronicle, Oakland public works director Harold Duffey said most discarded trash at illegal sites came from housed residents, who tossed their garbage at encampment sites rather than taking it to the dump. He noted that the problem has escalated every year since 2017 and that homeless residents “don’t generate much more than normal everyday trash.” 
 Now an ambassador, TJ Williams earns a $100 a week stipend to monitor cleaned areas and report new dumping. “It’s kinda not fair because we get blamed for it,” he says. “If we could keep all of this clean, it could really make a difference in how we’re seen.”

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: More trash than ever is washing up on Halifax's McNabs Island, groups says | CBC News
[METADATA/INTRO]: More trash than ever is washing up on Halifax's McNabs Island, groups says 800 bags of trash, 80 lobster traps were collected during the island's 30th annual cleanup on Sunday...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: The island is a magnet for garbage because it's situated at the mouth of the Halifax harbour, collecting trash from the city and the ocean. 
 The Friends of McNabs Island Society held its 30th annual cleanup on Sunday, which was postponed for nearly two years due to the COVID-19 pandemic. 
 McCarthy said 90 volunteers collected about 800 bags of garbage — a significant increase over previous years. 
 She said that much of an increase is concerning, despite the two-year delay in cleanups. 
 "We've been doing these cleanups for 30 years, so we pretty much know what to expect," she said.
